channel.title: NYT > Business > Energy & Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Why Little Was Done to Head Off Oil’s Strait of Hormuz Problem
item.description: Geography and regional rivalries have prevented Gulf countries from finding a true alternative to the strait, which the war with Iran has effectively shut down.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sahar Al Attar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A naval vessel sailing this month in the Strait of Hormuz. The waterway remains the only option to export most of the energy produced in the region.

item.title: Surge in Oil Prices Shakes Pakistan’s Already Fragile Economy
item.description: Families preparing for Eid al-Fitr and farmers ready for harvest are being squeezed in a country that gets nearly all its oil through the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A fuel storage site in Lahore, Pakistan, on Tuesday.

item.title: U.S. Vows to Block Iran’s Attempt to Shut Down Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz were “something we are dealing with.” And about 2,500 Marines were headed to the Middle East to bolster the war effort.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Luke Broadwater and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands marched amid airstrikes on Quds Day, a government-sponsored event, on Friday in Tehran.

item.title: Trump’s Move to Seize Oil Tankers Costs the U.S. Tens of Millions of Dollars
item.description: Although President Trump said seizing tankers would be a financial boon, the cost of maintaining just one aging ship has already reached $47 million.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Nicholas Nehamas
item.media:description: The oil tanker Skipper, seized by the United States, seen off the coast of Texas near Galveston in December.

item.title: Palisades Fire Recovery Tests L.A.’s Ability to Invest in Resilience
item.description: Palisades fire victims want to raise money for disaster hardening. Their idea could be a model — if it can get past L.A.’s most vexing housing problems.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Stephen Ross Goldstein for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marcela Silva at the site of her former Pacific Palisades home.

item.title: Surging Energy Costs Put German Industry ‘Really in Danger’
item.description: Four years after the Russian invasion of Ukraine sent energy prices soaring, the war in Iran is posing another challenge to efforts to revive European factories.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson and Andreas Meichsner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Carbon Caps and Emissions Trading Programs

item.title: Trump Removes Sanctions on Russia to Help Oil Flow Amid Iran Conflict
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said it was “unfortunate” that the move could benefit Russia, but maintained that it was only for the short term.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Thursday that Russia would not benefit significantly from the sanctions relief but acknowledged that it would see some financial benefit.

item.title: Trump Administration Sues California Over Tailpipe Emissions Limits
item.description: A lawsuit argues that the state’s regulations would illegally force a rapid transition to electric vehicles.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: The Trump administration has systematically moved to slash federal support for electric vehicles.

item.title: Countries Scramble to Shore Up Global Oil Supplies as Ships Are Attacked
item.description: The United States and Israel launched more strikes against Iran, where crowds mourned military commanders killed in the war. Israel also bombed targets in Lebanon, where the death toll climbed.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott, Abdi Latif Dahir, Eric Schmitt and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A navy ship from the United Arab Emirates sailed next to a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday.

item.title: World Leaders Will Release 400 Million Barrels of Oil to Stabilize Prices
item.description: The members of the International Energy Agency will release 400 million barrels of oil, the largest such coordinated action on record.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Iran Shocks Could Spur a Shift to Clean Energy — But Also to Coal
item.description: The oil crisis in the Middle East could spur countries to invest in wind, solar and otherrenewables. It could also spike reliance on coal, a cheap and polluting fossil fuel.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A plume of smoke from Israeli-U. S. airstrikes on Tehran on March 2.

item.title: How Trump and His Advisers Miscalculated Iran’s Response to War
item.description: In the lead-up to the U.S.-Israeli attack, President Trump downplayed the risks to the energy markets as a short-term concern that should not overshadow the mission to decapitate the Iranian regime.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: In response to Iranian threats, commercial shipping has come to a standstill in the Gulf, and oil prices have spiked.

item.title: Higher Oil Prices Could Put the Fed in a Bind as Labor Market Softens
item.description: Investors now expect that the Fed will delay a rate cut until September instead of July, as they had before the war in Iran began.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Economists expect energy-related price pressures to push inflation up to some degree in the coming months.

item.title: Rising Fuel Costs Pressure Airlines and Truckers
item.description: The price of jet fuel and diesel has surged since the war in Iran began, which could force airlines and trucking companies to pass on higher costs to their customers.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi, Emmett Lindner and Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The cost of jet fuel has reached record highs since the U.S.-led war with Iran began.

item.title: U.S. Solar Installations Fell in 2025 as Trump Attacked Clean Energy
item.description: More solar energy was added to U.S. grids than any other technology, but the amount installed fell by 14 percent, according to a new report.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Mason Trinca for The New York Times
item.media:description: A solar field in Riesel, Texas, in 2023. Solar power is projected to account for just over half of the new power projects that will be added this year.

item.title: Oil Prices Will Remain High as Iran War Continues
item.description: While officials look for ways to ease oil shocks, experts say higher prices will likely persist until traffic through the Strait of Hormuz returns.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Black smoke filled the skies above Tehran on Sunday morning after Israeli airstrikes struck energy facilities overnight.

item.title: G7 Countries Hold Off on Releasing Emergency Oil Supplies
item.description: Finance ministers for the seven industrialized countries met on Monday and said they would consider releasing oil from reserves but were not ready to do so now.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Aurelien Breeden
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Smoke rose from a refinery operated by Bapco Energies, Bahrain’s state-owned energy company, on Monday. Global oil prices have spiked since the U.S.-led war with Iran began.

item.title: ‘The Old Game Is Back’: Oil Is a Potent Geopolitical Tool Again
item.description: The war in the Middle East has sent prices soaring, showing how much the world continues to depend on reliable supplies of oil and gas.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil tanker off the coast of El Segundo, Calif., last week. The United States is the world’s biggest producer of oil and natural gas but is still feeling the effects of the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Oil Prices Spike to Over $110 a Barrel, Highest Since Pandemic
item.description: The jump was a sign of growing concern that the war in the Middle East will take a toll on energy supplies.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The price of a gallon of regular gasoline has climbed about 16 percent since the United States and Israel attacked Iran on Feb. 28.

item.title: Defense Without U.S. Help Is a Live Topic for Canada, Japan and Australia
item.description: The leaders of Japan and Canada are making a unified front on defense cooperation as President Trump raises the pressure over military spending.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Adrian Wyld/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving in Tokyo on Friday as part of a tour that also included Australia.
